author: demchy19
content: For swings, do you usually watch stocks like Jack and Tony, or do you have a broader variety of others that you monitor?

author: Kam
content: i have a watchlist and scanning process that have built over time that is pretty broad (about 50 tickers). I go thru this every night...helps keeps me in tune with what's happening with the market. that being said; i'll usually only actively trade a handful (like 5). e.g. for the past couple months have mostly just been in TSLA, MSTR, META, PLTR and TQQQ.  I'm new to options and relatively new to daytrading, but have had consistency swing trading with shares. learning so much from this community so trying to dabble in swinging options - it's definitely different from shares lol 😂

author: demchy19
content:
Oh, that’s great! 😊 Do you have any good tips or tricks to share? LOL! I’m still pretty new to trading on a live account. so any tips are welcome 😄  
Stocks are great for beginners, but they definitely demand a bigger account, which most people don’t have. I’m not really interested in trading penny stocks, so I’ve started getting familiar with options instead. They’re definitely riskier, but I think if you manage the risk well, they can be a great option.

author: Kam
content: yea i totally agree. Options are a fantastic instrument, but there's definitely a learning curve (finding that out the hard way haha). honestly I think Tony & Jack are awesome teachers, and their "Top-Down" approach is exactly how I learned to swing trade (except I learned on weekly and daily charts only). drilling down further to get even lower risk entries creates even better opps IMO. <@801169196015943731> premarket prep sessions are SO good and a great way to build HTF thesis which is seems like you're already doing great with. I wouldn't worry about learning new swing techniques and just keep doing what these guys are teaching - keep it simple! If i had to give one tip it's to ride the 9/21 EMAs on daily charts in strong markets. The hardest part about swing trading is that you can't do it all the time - most of the time markets are consolidating; so when you get a hot market like we currently have - gotta take advantage

author: demchy19
content: That makes a lot of sense. I understand what you're looking for. But in my opinion, based on my experience, I’d like to focus more on swing trading, but not with options... at least not yet. The losses on a small account with options are much worse. For swing trading, I’d switch to stocks so the risk is more manageable. Also, if the trade doesn’t go your way on Monday, you can still hold and maybe by Friday, it will move in your direction. But with options, you need to be very precise with the timing because even if the stock hits your target, if the option is close to expiration, you can still lose money. So for now, the plan is to day trade with options and focus on swing trading with stocks in the future when i will have some money LOL.

author: EeveeWinter
content: I’d scale some profits out of those swings, especially if they are deep in the money. You can let some of the rest of the position ride free. If the delta on some of your swing positions are nearing 1 (about 0.90) then at that point they start acting like the stock itself and you lose the leverage. Might as well reposition with the profits into more leverage.

author: EeveeWinter
content:
Pros of Selling the DITM Call and Rebuying:

Tighter Bid-Ask Spreads:
Deep ITM options often have wider spreads, making them expensive to trade. Replacing it with a more actively traded strike could reduce slippage.

Better Use of Capital (Higher Gamma/Vega):
DITM options behave almost like stock (delta ≈ 1), with very low time value and minimal leverage benefit. Rolling to a slightly OTM or ATM call might give you more exposure per dollar.
Free Up Time Value (Extrinsic Value):
If there’s still some extrinsic value left in the DITM call, you can sell it while that value still exists and roll into a more efficient or tactical option position.

Avoid Early Exercise Risk:
While rare, there’s always a small chance the option gets exercised early if it's American-style and deep ITM (especially near ex-dividend dates for calls).

author: CBD Steve
content:
NVDA Earnings plan - Fake out candle has happened last 3 quarters systematically between 4:10pm-4:20pm dropping $6-$9.

November, 20th 2024 - Fake out 10 minute candle: $137.92 - $146.24 (Good sell time 40 minutes after fake out) 9:20 price is $149.62

February, 26th 2025 - Fake out 10 minute candle: $125.58 - $136.59 (Ideal sell time 40 minutes after fake out) 9:20 price is $135.42 not higher than 40 minutes after

May, 28th 2025 - Fake out 10 minute candle: $133.29 - 139.99 (Ideal sell time 40 minutes after fake out) 9:20 price is $142.73 not higher than 40 minutes after

My plan: Wait for fake out to bottom out - confirm with volume chart. Buy 100 shares of NVDA. Butt clench for 40 minutes. 4:50-4:59pm sell all shares.

Fake out will drop $7-$10 historically (3 quarters data)

Buy price - only once confirmed reversal
Risk - Extremely high (no stops)
Reward - $500-$1000 profit on 100 shares
Plan B - Hold shares for 1-3 weeks if it needs to recover 
Research - About an hr lol
Additional help - I could only go back on earnings based on 10 minute candle so there is more I could be missing
Additional Discovery - At 4am 1 day before earnings it gaps up 1-3%. 2-3 quarters. It did gap down 1 quarter.

author: FierceTiger
content: TSLA is a high-conviction intraday short if it becomes red (i.e. crosses previous day's close) tomorrow, after this parabolic move up. It is the classic "First Red Day" setup. Short it with everything you got once price crosses from green to red, be out 80% of your position on the first green candle (first consolidation), and leave 20% runners (sell 10% EOD and leave 10% expecting Day 2 continuation).

author: FierceTiger
content: Point of invalidation (stop loss) is previous day's close, if it reclaims. But highly unlikely it reclaims on a "First Red Day" setup.

author: jayjonbeach
content:
Here is a lot more context and macro view of SPX & SPY.  This is the Monthly chart since 2009 with an Elliot Wave Technical Analysis count on it
It looks to be pretty close to fully cooked, but, EW analysis is infamous for being somewhat unreliable depending on who is constructing it 
Likely most won't know what the numbers mean, the main idea is impulse waves consist of 5 waves, 3 up and 2 corrective.  
Once 5 waves are done, the entire wave will be corrected.  You can see an example of this where the orange wave 3 was corrected with a Triangle
It can be very tricky trying to count the last part of an impulse, sometimes the market isn't quite done where it might look to be. 
Anyway something people might want to keep an eye on, I'm sure if market tanks Trump & the Tariffs will be the main blame, or maybe a new plandemic is coming
